stem:
A 32-year-old G3P2 woman at term is in second stage of labour in the birthing suite. Both previous deliveries were uncomplicated 4.0 kg babies. Her current estimated fetal weight by ultrasound at 38 weeks was 4.3 kg. Active second stage has been 25 minutes. The head has delivered but does not restitute, retracting back against the perineum with each contraction.

Attempts at gentle downward axial traction with the next contraction do not deliver the anterior shoulder. The midwife calls for assistance. You are the registrar; the consultant is en route. The mother is on a standard delivery bed and time of head delivery has just been called.

data_table.Vital signs: maternal vital signs within normal limits; fetal heart rate baseline 130 /min on intermittent auscultation
data_table.Examination: head delivered, turtle sign present, no shoulder rotation with traction
data_table.Investigations: intrapartum, no time for further investigations

lead_in: What is the single best initial manoeuvre after calling for help?

options.text: McRoberts manoeuvre with hyperflexion of the maternal hips onto the abdomen
options.rationale: McRoberts is the first manoeuvre in the HELPERR mnemonic for shoulder dystocia under RANZCOG and ALSO. It rotates the symphysis cephalad and increases the anteroposterior diameter of the pelvic outlet, resolving 40 to 60% of cases alone. Suprapubic pressure is added next if McRoberts fails. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

options.text: Fundal pressure now to help expel the impacted anterior shoulder
options.rationale: Fundal pressure worsens shoulder impaction and increases the risk of uterine rupture and brachial plexus injury. It is explicitly avoided in shoulder dystocia. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

options.text: Symphysiotomy under local anaesthetic to enlarge the pelvic outlet
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is a desperate last-resort manoeuvre after all internal manoeuvres have failed and Zavanelli is not feasible. It has high maternal morbidity. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

options.text: Apply continued strong axial traction with each subsequent contraction
options.rationale: Strong traction risks brachial plexus injury and does not address impaction. Routine traction is gentle and axial only; force is not the solution. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

options.text: Zavanelli manoeuvre with replacement of the head and immediate caesarean delivery
options.rationale: Zavanelli is a last-resort option after all other internal manoeuvres have failed. It requires uterine relaxation and theatre. McRoberts comes first. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia is an obstetric emergency requiring a structured response. The HELPERR mnemonic provides the sequence: call for Help, evaluate for Episiotomy if needed, Legs (McRoberts), Pressure (suprapubic), Enter for internal rotation (Rubin, Wood screw, reverse Wood screw), Remove the posterior arm, Roll the mother onto all fours (Gaskin). McRoberts is the first and most effective single manoeuvre. Fundal pressure is contraindicated. Brachial plexus injury and hypoxia drive morbidity; documentation, debriefing, and risk management are essential post-event.
key_points: HELPERR: Help, Episiotomy, Legs, Pressure, Enter, Remove arm, Roll
McRoberts alone resolves 40 to 60% of shoulder dystocias
Fundal pressure is contraindicated; it worsens impaction
Document head-to-body interval, manoeuvres used, and order of use
explanation.pearls: Shoulder dystocia HELPERR: McRoberts hyperflexion plus suprapubic pressure first; never fundal pressure. McRoberts alone resolves up to 60%. Debrief and document timing of every manoeuvre.

stem:
A 31-year-old G2P1 woman at 36+5 weeks attends antenatal clinic having been told at her routine appointment last week that the baby was breech. The previous delivery was an uncomplicated vaginal birth of a cephalic baby at 39 weeks. She has no significant past medical history and the current pregnancy has been uncomplicated.

Today's ultrasound confirms frank breech presentation with an estimated fetal weight on the 60th centile, normal liquor volume (deepest vertical pocket 5 cm), anterior placenta not low lying, and reassuring umbilical artery Doppler. The CTG performed on arrival is reassuring. There is no history of antepartum haemorrhage, no uterine scar, and she has not yet decided on her birth plan.

data_table.Vital signs: maternal vital signs within normal limits; fetal heart rate baseline 138 /min, reactive CTG
data_table.Examination: soft non-tender abdomen, fundal height 36 cm, breech palpable in the pelvis, fetal heart auscultated in upper quadrant
data_table.Investigations: ultrasound: frank breech, EFW 60th centile, DVP 5 cm, anterior placenta not low lying, UA Doppler normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer for moxibustion at acupuncture points BL67 as the first intervention
options.rationale: Moxibustion has weak and inconsistent evidence; it is an adjunct rather than a first-line intervention under RANZCOG. ECV is the recommended primary offer. (Source: RANZCOG Management of Breech Presentation)

options.text: Book elective caesarean section at 39 weeks without offering ECV
options.rationale: Defaulting to caesarean section without offering ECV does not meet RANZCOG standards. ECV should be discussed as it can convert to cephalic presentation, allowing vaginal birth in many cases. (Source: RANZCOG Management of Breech Presentation)

options.text: Recommend immediate vaginal breech delivery planning given multiparity
options.rationale: Planned vaginal breech birth has higher perinatal morbidity than planned caesarean (Term Breech Trial); it is an option only after detailed risk counselling and in select cases with experienced operators. It is not the default in a multiparous woman without an attempt at ECV. (Source: RANZCOG Management of Breech Presentation)

options.text: Trial a knee-to-chest position three times daily and review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Postural manoeuvres have not been shown to convert breech presentation in meta-analyses. They delay ECV during the optimal window. (Source: RANZCOG Management of Breech Presentation)

options.text: Offer external cephalic version at 37 weeks with tocolysis after counselling
options.rationale: RANZCOG recommends offering ECV from 36 weeks for nulliparous women and 37 weeks for multiparous women with breech presentation, reassuring fetal welfare, no contraindications, and normal liquor. Success rates are around 50% (higher in multiparous women with adequate liquor) and serious complications such as placental abruption are rare. Tocolysis with subcutaneous terbutaline improves success. (Source: RANZCOG Management of Breech Presentation)

explanation.summary: RANZCOG recommends offering external cephalic version from 36 weeks (nulliparous) or 37 weeks (multiparous) to women with breech presentation at term, with reassuring fetal status and no contraindications (significant antepartum haemorrhage, scarred uterus, oligohydramnios, abnormal CTG, multiple pregnancy after first twin). Tocolysis with subcutaneous terbutaline 250 micrograms improves success. Counselling covers success rates (around 50%, higher in multiparous women), the small risk of emergency caesarean for non-reassuring CTG or abruption, and the alternatives of planned vaginal breech or planned caesarean. Anti-D is given to RhD-negative women.
key_points: Offer ECV from 36 weeks (nulliparous) or 37 weeks (multiparous)
Success around 50%, higher in multiparous women with adequate liquor
Tocolysis with subcutaneous terbutaline 250 micrograms improves success
Give anti-D 625 IU to RhD-negative women after ECV
explanation.pearls: Term breech RANZCOG: offer ECV from 36 weeks (nulliparous) or 37 weeks (multiparous) with tocolysis (subcut terbutaline 250 micrograms). Success around 50%; anti-D 625 IU if RhD-negative.

stem:
A 27-year-old G2P1 woman at 28+4 weeks presents to the birthing unit with a sudden gush of clear fluid one hour ago, followed by ongoing leaking. There are no contractions, no per vaginal bleeding, and fetal movements are normal. Her previous pregnancy ended in spontaneous vaginal delivery at term.

Sterile speculum confirms pooling of clear fluid in the posterior fornix. Cervix appears closed; no digital examination is performed. Maternal observations and CTG are reassuring. Ultrasound shows oligohydramnios with a deepest vertical pocket of 1.8 cm; estimated fetal weight is on the 45th centile; the fetus is cephalic. There are no clinical features of chorioamnionitis (no fever, uterine tenderness, or offensive discharge).

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 88 /min, blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, respiratory rate 16 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: no contractions, soft non-tender uterus, sterile speculum shows pooling of clear amniotic fluid
data_table.Investigations: FBC WCC 9.8 x10^9/L (4 to 11); CRP 6 mg/L; high vaginal swab and urine culture sent; CTG reassuring; group B streptococcus status unknown

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management bundle?

options.text: Induce labour immediately with oxytocin to minimise infection risk
options.rationale: Active induction before 34 weeks in the absence of chorioamnionitis, fetal compromise, or other indication for delivery harms neonatal outcomes through prematurity. Expectant management with prophylactic antibiotics is the standard. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes Guideline

options.text: Admit for IM betamethasone, oral erythromycin, and IV magnesium for neuroprotection
options.rationale: PPROM under 34 weeks without chorioamnionitis is managed expectantly with antenatal corticosteroids (IM betamethasone 11.4 mg, two doses 24 hours apart), prophylactic erythromycin for 10 days (or until delivery) to prolong latency, and magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection when delivery within 24 hours is anticipated or below 30 weeks. The ORACLE trial supports erythromycin over co-amoxiclav, which increased neonatal necrotising enterocolitis. (Source: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes Guideline

options.text: Admit, give a single dose of dexamethasone, and start IV co-amoxiclav for 10 days
options.rationale: Co-amoxiclav is contraindicated for prophylaxis in PPROM by ORACLE because of increased neonatal necrotising enterocolitis. Two doses of corticosteroid are required, not one, to achieve the surfactant effect. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes Guideline

options.text: Discharge home on oral metronidazole with daily fetal movement counting
options.rationale: PPROM warrants inpatient observation for chorioamnionitis, fetal welfare, and cord prolapse risk. Metronidazole is not the prophylactic agent of choice and outpatient management is not first-line under RANZCOG. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes Guideline

options.text: Perform a digital cervical examination to plan timing of induction
options.rationale: Digital examination in PPROM increases infection risk and is avoided unless labour is established or imminent. Sterile speculum is sufficient for assessment. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes Guideline

explanation.summary: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes under 34 weeks without chorioamnionitis is managed expectantly. Admission allows monitoring for infection, abruption, and fetal compromise. Antenatal corticosteroids (IM betamethasone 11.4 mg, two doses 24 hours apart) accelerate fetal lung maturity. Prophylactic erythromycin 250 mg four times daily for 10 days (or until delivery) prolongs latency. Magnesium sulfate is given for neuroprotection if delivery is anticipated within 24 hours or below 30 weeks. Digital cervical examination is avoided to reduce infection risk.
key_points: Antenatal corticosteroids: two doses of IM betamethasone 11.4 mg 24 hours apart
Prophylactic erythromycin 250 mg four times daily for 10 days (ORACLE); co-amoxiclav contraindicated
Magnesium for neuroprotection if delivery anticipated within 24 hours or below 30 weeks
Avoid digital cervical examination; use sterile speculum
explanation.pearls: PPROM under 34 weeks without chorioamnionitis: betamethasone 11.4 mg x 2 doses 24 hours apart, erythromycin 250 mg qid for 10 days (NOT co-amoxiclav per ORACLE), magnesium for neuroprotection if delivery imminent or under 30 weeks. Avoid digital exam.

sources.label: RANZCOG Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes Guideline

stem:
A 28-year-old man with first-episode psychosis was commenced on olanzapine 10 mg nightly six weeks ago by the early psychosis team. His psychotic symptoms have settled, his sleep has improved, and he is back at his apprenticeship. He has gained 3 kg since starting and is generally well. He attends your community mental health clinic for routine review.

Baseline bloods taken when olanzapine was started showed fasting glucose 4.8 mmol/L (3.9 to 5.4), HbA1c 5.2%, total cholesterol 4.2 mmol/L, LDL 2.4 mmol/L, triglycerides 1.0 mmol/L. ECG showed a QTc of 410 ms. Weight at baseline was 78 kg; today 81 kg. There is no personal or family history of diabetes.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 76 /min, blood pressure 124/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 14 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: BMI 27 kg/m^2; no signs of EPS, akathisia, or tardive dyskinesia
data_table.Investigations: ongoing weekly weight checks since commencement

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next monitoring schedule?

options.text: Recheck metabolic bloods only if he gains more than 7% of his baseline weight
options.rationale: Weight gain triggers extra review, but routine 12-week metabolic monitoring is scheduled regardless of weight under RANZCP. Many patients develop dyslipidaemia or insulin resistance before substantial weight change. (Source: RANZCP Adult Antipsychotic Monitoring Statement)

options.text: Repeat fasting glucose, lipids, and weight at 12 weeks then yearly thereafter
options.rationale: RANZCP and the Maudsley Guidelines recommend baseline, 12-week, and then yearly metabolic monitoring (fasting glucose or HbA1c, lipids, weight, BP, waist circumference) for all patients on second-generation antipsychotics, with more frequent weight checks during the first three months. Olanzapine and clozapine have the highest metabolic burden of the second-generation agents. (Source: RANZCP Adult Antipsychotic Monitoring Statement)

options.text: Switch to quetiapine to reduce metabolic risk and recheck at one year
options.rationale: Switching is not indicated in a patient stable and responding well after 6 weeks of olanzapine. Monitoring is the next step; switching is reserved for significant or intolerable metabolic decline despite lifestyle intervention. (Source: RANZCP Adult Antipsychotic Monitoring Statement)

options.text: Add metformin 500 mg twice daily prophylactically to prevent metabolic syndrome
options.rationale: Metformin is considered for olanzapine- or clozapine-associated weight gain after it occurs and lifestyle measures fail; it is not routinely prescribed prophylactically at week 6 in a normoglycaemic patient. (Source: RANZCP Adult Antipsychotic Monitoring Statement)

options.text: Repeat the ECG every 3 months indefinitely while he remains on olanzapine
options.rationale: Routine 3-monthly ECG is not required for olanzapine. ECG is checked at baseline and repeated if dose-related QTc concerns arise or with high-QTc agents like ziprasidone. The metabolic profile is the priority for olanzapine. (Source: RANZCP Adult Antipsychotic Monitoring Statement)

explanation.summary: RANZCP, the Maudsley Guidelines, and Therapeutic Guidelines Psychotropic all recommend a structured metabolic monitoring schedule for second-generation antipsychotics: baseline, 12 weeks, then yearly for fasting glucose or HbA1c, fasting lipids, weight, waist circumference, and BP. Olanzapine and clozapine carry the highest metabolic burden; aripiprazole and lurasidone the lowest. Lifestyle counselling starts at initiation; metformin can be added in established or progressive weight gain or pre-diabetes. ECG is baseline plus reassessment with QT-prolonging agents.
key_points: Baseline, 12-week, then yearly metabolic monitoring is RANZCP standard
Weight checks more frequent (often weekly to monthly) in the first 3 months
Olanzapine and clozapine carry the highest metabolic burden
Metformin for weight gain or pre-diabetes after lifestyle intervention; not prophylactic
explanation.pearls: Second-gen antipsychotic monitoring: baseline, 12-week, then yearly fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipids, weight, BP, waist. Weight checks more often in first 3 months. Metformin for established weight gain or pre-diabetes after lifestyle intervention.

stem:
A 52-year-old woman with chronic schizophrenia stable on aripiprazole attends your community mental health clinic. She has smoked 25 cigarettes per day since age 18 and wants to quit because of new chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. She has tried nicotine patches twice in the past with limited success.

Her mental state is stable. She has no current depressive features, no suicidal thoughts, and no recent psychotic relapse. She has no past history of seizures or anorexia. She has no significant cardiovascular history. She is keen on a more aggressive pharmacological approach this time, supported by her case manager. She has no contraindications to systemic medication on review of her current chart.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 78 /min, blood pressure 132/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 16 /min, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 36.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: no nicotine staining of fingers visible (she wears gloves), no oral mucosal lesions
data_table.Investigations: spirometry confirms COPD; FEV1 65% predicted; FBC, UEC, LFT within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Bupropion SR 150 mg daily increasing to 150 mg twice daily after a week
options.rationale: Bupropion is effective but carries a seizure risk in eating disorders and uncontrolled epilepsy, and has more drug interactions than varenicline. Varenicline has stronger evidence in stable schizophrenia per EAGLES. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guideline)

options.text: Varenicline 1 mg twice daily after one-week escalation, quit date in week 2
options.rationale: Varenicline has the highest abstinence rates in the RACGP Smoking Cessation Guideline and is suitable in stable psychiatric illness. The EAGLES trial showed no excess neuropsychiatric adverse events compared with placebo, patch, or bupropion. Dose escalates over week 1 (0.5 mg daily x3 days, 0.5 mg bd x4 days, then 1 mg bd) with a target quit date in week 2. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guideline)

options.text: Combination patch plus oral nicotine replacement therapy with behavioural support
options.rationale: Combination NRT is a reasonable option and is also first-line in RACGP, but in a 25-a-day smoker with prior patch failure and stable psychiatric illness, varenicline has the greatest abstinence effect. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guideline)

options.text: Off-label nortriptyline 75 mg nightly for smoking cessation support
options.rationale: Nortriptyline is second-line at best and not registered for smoking cessation in Australia. Side effects (anticholinergic, QT) limit utility. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guideline)

options.text: Clonidine 75 micrograms twice daily titrated to symptoms of nicotine withdrawal
options.rationale: Clonidine has weak evidence for smoking cessation and is not recommended as first-line. Hypotension and sedation limit acceptability. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guideline)

explanation.summary: First-line pharmacotherapy for tobacco smoking cessation in Australia includes varenicline, combination nicotine replacement therapy (patch plus short-acting), and bupropion. Varenicline has the highest abstinence effect in network meta-analyses and is now considered safe in stable psychiatric illness following the EAGLES trial. Dose escalates over week 1 with a target quit date in week 2; treatment continues for 12 weeks. Behavioural support (Quitline, brief intervention) doubles efficacy. Bupropion is avoided in eating disorders and uncontrolled epilepsy. NRT alone is appropriate for lighter smokers and where varenicline is contraindicated.
key_points: Varenicline 1 mg twice daily after week-1 escalation; 12-week course
EAGLES trial: no excess neuropsychiatric adverse events in stable psychiatric illness
Bupropion avoided in eating disorders and uncontrolled epilepsy
Combination NRT (patch plus short-acting) is also first-line
explanation.pearls: Smoking cessation first-line: varenicline 1 mg twice daily (after week-1 escalation), combination NRT, or bupropion. EAGLES showed varenicline is safe in stable psychiatric illness. Always pair with Quitline or behavioural support.

stem:
A 23-year-old woman is referred to your community mental health team following a third presentation in 12 weeks for superficial wrist lacerations after arguments with her partner. She describes intense rage, chronic emptiness, and frantic efforts to avoid being left when relationships strain. She has had three boyfriends and two best friends in two years.

Her mood swings throughout each day between euphoric and tearful. There are no fixed delusions, no command hallucinations, no manic episodes lasting beyond an afternoon, and no full depressive episodes. She has tried two SSRIs over 18 months with limited benefit. She has no substance dependence currently. The team formulation lands on borderline personality disorder by DSM-5-TR criteria.

data_table.Examination: mental state: dysphoric, intermittently tearful, reactive affect, no psychosis, no current suicidal intent though chronic suicidal ideation

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Switch the SSRI to venlafaxine extended release as an alternative antidepressant trial
options.rationale: Antidepressants help comorbid depression but do not target BPD symptoms specifically. Repeated antidepressant switching in the absence of a clear depressive episode does not address the core problem. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg nightly to stabilise mood and behaviour
options.rationale: Antipsychotics are not first-line in BPD and have modest effects on a narrow symptom range (cognitive perceptual, anger). NHMRC explicitly cautions against using them as a substitute for structured psychotherapy. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder

options.text: Commence sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily for mood stabilisation
options.rationale: Sodium valproate has weak and inconsistent evidence in BPD; it is teratogenic and not appropriate for a young woman of reproductive age unless pregnancy is reliably prevented. NHMRC does not endorse it as first-line. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder

options.text: Plan a brief inpatient admission to contain the behaviour after each episode
options.rationale: Brief crisis-driven admissions can reinforce dependence on inpatient care without addressing core deficits. NHMRC supports community-based structured psychotherapy and only short admissions for acute risk that cannot otherwise be managed. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder

options.text: Refer for a structured psychological therapy such as dialectical behaviour therapy
options.rationale: RANZCP and NHMRC guidance identify structured psychological therapies (dialectical behaviour therapy, mentalisation-based therapy, schema-focused therapy, transference-focused therapy) as first-line for borderline personality disorder. Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive for specific symptom clusters, never primary. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder

explanation.summary: Borderline personality disorder is treated primarily with structured psychological therapy under the NHMRC guideline, RANZCP, and NICE. Evidence-based options include dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT), mentalisation-based therapy (MBT), schema-focused therapy (SFT), and transference-focused psychotherapy (TFP). Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive, used for specific comorbidities (depression, psychosis, mood instability) and discontinued if not clearly helpful. Admission is reserved for acute risk that cannot otherwise be managed safely.
key_points: Structured psychological therapy is first-line in BPD (DBT, MBT, SFT, TFP)
Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive, targeting specific symptom clusters only
Avoid valproate in women of reproductive age unless contraception is reliable
Brief planned admissions for crisis only; avoid long inpatient stays
explanation.pearls: BPD first-line: structured psychological therapy (DBT, MBT, schema, TFP). Pharmacotherapy adjunctive only. Avoid valproate in women of reproductive age. Crisis admissions kept brief and planned.

sources.label: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder
